title: My single most useful tip for agentic coding workflows
author: Robert Sahlin
text:
My single most useful tip for agentic coding workflows
The self-correction loop and the scope creep buffer
Since the release of gemini-cli I have spent quite some time exploring agentic coding workflows with a human in the loop. I’ve tried a few different frameworks like taskmaster-ai and spec-kit, and even built my own framework with custom MCPs.
Regardless of which framework I use, there is one specific thing I do to improve the outcome over time.
Whenever I see the agent providing the wrong output, struggling to solve a problem, or if I need to manually steer it back on track, I don’t just fix the code. I ask the agent to help improve itself.
I usually ask something like: “What kind of instruction do you need to add to your context file in order to avoid this in the future?”
I then add that instruction to the system prompt. A concrete example of this is how I now handle scope creep.
I noticed that the agent sometimes started implementing things that were not part of the current task, or it would ignore good architectural improvements because they were “out of scope”. So I used my self-correction method and asked the agent how to solve this.
The result was that the agent wrote this specific instruction for my Gemini.md context file:
6. Tracking Future Improvements
When an out-of-scope finding or a potential improvement is identified, it must be tracked for future implementation. This workflow ensures that good ideas are not lost.
1. Identify and Articulate: Clearly define the improvement with a title, description, location, and a high-level implementation suggestion.
2. Log the Finding: Add the finding to the FUTURE_IMPROVEMENTS.md file in the root of the project. This file serves as our central log.
We then review this file together to plan future iterations, keeping the current context clean and focused. Now, instead of getting distracted, the agent helps me collect future features automatically.
P.S. A note on data quality
I am also writing a post right now about a data health engine we have implemented at Nordnet. We wanted to make the state of our data platform completely transparent for our end users.
We built a solution entirely on GCP services like Cloud Run and BigQuery that keeps track of data quality issues and incidents. It calculates the blast radius of an incident, the downstream data assets impacted and we even surface the status as a “data health badge” directly in the Looker dashboards.
I will publish the full technical implementation details soon, so keep an eye out for that.
